text:
Jamaica's sprinting prodigy, Nickecoy Bramwell, will not be competing in theParis 2024 Olympics,despite breaking a record previously held byUsain Bolt.The Caribbean nation has found it challenging to maintain its supremacy in sprinting since the retirement of Bolt, an eight-time Olympic champion and widely considered the greatest sprinter ever.

After failing to secure a medal in the men's 100m and 200m at the Tokyo Olympics, Jamaica's next generation of sprinters, including Oblique Seville, Ackeem Blake, and Kishane Thompson, are set to represent the country in Paris. Expectations are high, particularly for Thompson, who became this year's fastest man in the 100m after clocking a personal best of 9.77 seconds at the Jamaican Olympic trials.

However, they will have to compete without 16-year-old sensation Bramwell. The teenager, who will miss his chance to debut at the Paris Olympics,gained recognition in May when he broke Bolt's Under-17 400m record at the 51st Carifta Games.

Discovery+is the new home of the Olympics. The streamer is set to broadcast 3,800 hours of sport live from Paris.

Sports fans can getDiscovery+ for £3.99a month until the end of 2024 via Amazon Prime's video channels. This also comes with anAmazon Prime 30-day free trial.

Bramwell narrowly beat Bolt's 22-year-old record of 47.33 seconds, set in 2002, with an impressive time of 47.26 seconds at the Kirani James Athletics Stadium in Grenada. Upon obliterating the long-standing record, the athlete said: "It's a wonderful feeling to break the record.

"Since last summer, I have been eyeing the record, so it's a great feeling I could come out here and get it, I just took my mind off it and focused on the record." Defying belief, given Jamaica's reputation for producing fleet-footed stars, Bramwell openly shared with the Jamaican newspaper, the Gleaner, thatthere was a time he wasn't even the quickest in his class.



During an exchange concerning his athletic inspiration, it is hardly surprising that Bolt emerged as his idol. He further discussed how critical his family has been to both his personal life and burgeoning track career.

"It's a great feeling, you know, to represent your country and to win for your country," he said. "My family is always there. They're always at my track meets since the season has started. I can't really answer that now about medals, but the season has been good. No, I don't feel any pressure at all for this Championship really. It's just a great feeling to represent Calabar."

title: Teen who broke Usain Bolt record not in Jamaican sprint team | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Fasika Zelealem
description: Jamaica have named their top three sprinters for the Paris Olympics - but one notable runner is missing despite breaking a record held by Usain Bolt.
